title: Unsafe Dose Order & High-Alert Medication Safety
subtitle: 5 y/o, 20 kg — an ordered dose that exceeds the safe range
nursesNote: 1300 - 5 y/o child weighing 20 kg (44 lb) admitted with a febrile illness. A new order reads: 'acetaminophen 600 mg PO now.' The unit's pediatric medication reference lists the recommended acetaminophen dose as 10-15 mg/kg per dose. The nurse notes the order was entered shortly after a verbal handoff and that the same prescriber's earlier orders used a trailing zero (e.g., '1.0 mg'). The child has no known liver disease and normal baseline liver enzymes. The nurse has not yet administered the dose.

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: Identify what makes this order unsafe
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) findings that indicate this order should NOT be given as written.

opts.text: The safe range for this 20 kg child is 200-300 mg per dose (10-15 mg/kg)
opts.rat: Multiplying 10-15 mg/kg by 20 kg gives a safe single dose of 200-300 mg-the reference the nurse must check the order against.

opts.text: The ordered 600 mg equals 30 mg/kg, about double the maximum safe single dose
opts.rat: 600 mg divided by 20 kg is 30 mg/kg, well above the 10-15 mg/kg range-an unsafe dose that must be questioned.

opts.text: The order followed a verbal handoff and the prescriber uses trailing zeros, both error-prone practices
opts.rat: Verbal orders and trailing zeros ('1.0 mg' misread as '10 mg') are recognized sources of tenfold medication errors, raising suspicion of a transcription error.

opts.text: The child has a temperature of 102.4F
opts.rat: The fever is the indication for the medication, not evidence that the dose is unsafe.

opts.text: Baseline ALT and AST are within normal limits
opts.rat: Normal liver enzymes are reassuring, but they do not make an excessive dose safe.

opts.text: Heart rate of 124 in a febrile 5-year-old
opts.rat: Tachycardia is expected with fever in a young child; it is not a cue about the dose.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: Interpret the risk and the safeguards
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) statements that correctly interpret this situation.

opts.text: Acetaminophen given above recommended limits risks liver (hepatic) injury
opts.rat: Exceeding recommended acetaminophen dosing is a well-established cause of hepatotoxicity, which is why the safe range matters.

opts.text: The nurse is accountable for verifying a dose is safe before administering it, even when a prescriber ordered it
opts.rat: Administering a medication is an independent nursing responsibility; a nurse who gives a clearly unsafe dose shares accountability for the harm.

opts.text: Error-prone abbreviations such as 'U' for units, trailing zeros, and missing leading zeros should be avoided in orders
opts.rat: These notations are on 'do not use' lists precisely because they cause tenfold dosing errors; orders using them should be clarified.

opts.text: The nurse should give the dose as written because the prescriber is responsible for the order
opts.rat: Following a clearly unsafe order does not transfer accountability; the nurse must clarify before administering.

opts.text: A dose only needs to be checked if the medication is on the high-alert list
opts.rat: Every dose is verified; high-alert medications simply add an extra independent double-check requirement.

opts.text: Weight-based dosing is unnecessary in children if the child looks average sized
opts.rat: Pediatric doses must be calculated from an accurate measured weight, never estimated by appearance.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Most likely explanation for the discrepancy
steps.inst: Drag to rank the explanations from MOST likely (1) to LEAST likely (5).

opts.text: A transcription/decimal error during order entry after the verbal handoff
opts.rat: A dose that is a clean multiple of the safe dose, entered after a verbal handoff by a prescriber using error-prone notation, most suggests a transcription error.

opts.text: The order was intended as a total daily amount rather than a single dose
opts.rat: Confusing a daily total with a single dose is another common ordering error worth clarifying.

opts.text: The dose was calculated from an incorrect (adult or estimated) weight
opts.rat: Using a wrong weight is a plausible source of pediatric dosing error and should be checked.

opts.text: The prescriber deliberately intends a dose above the standard range
opts.rat: Unlikely for routine fever management, and it would still require explicit clarification before administering.

opts.text: The pediatric reference range in the unit manual is wrong
opts.rat: Least likely: standard references are well established, so the order-not the reference-is the thing to question.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Select the FOUR (4) MOST appropriate actions.

opts.text: Hold the dose and do not administer it as written
opts.rat: A dose outside the safe range is withheld until it is clarified; giving it first and asking later risks harm.

opts.text: Recalculate using the child's documented weight and the reference range
opts.rat: Independently recalculating confirms the discrepancy before the prescriber is contacted.

opts.text: Contact the prescriber to clarify and obtain a corrected written order
opts.rat: Clarifying with the prescriber and getting a corrected, clearly written order resolves the problem at its source.

opts.text: Ask a second nurse for an independent double-check of the calculation
opts.rat: An independent double-check-standard for high-alert medications and useful for any questionable dose-catches calculation errors.

opts.text: Give half the ordered dose so it falls closer to the safe range
opts.rat: Nurses do not unilaterally alter a prescribed dose; the order must be clarified and rewritten.

opts.text: Administer the 600 mg and document that the order seemed high
opts.rat: Giving a known unsafe dose and documenting concern afterward does not protect the child.

opts.text: Accept a verbal correction without a read-back or a written order
opts.rat: Verbal orders require read-back verification and prompt written entry; accepting one without either repeats the original error risk.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Drag to rank the actions from FIRST (1) to LAST (5).

opts.text: Hold the medication and do not administer
opts.rat: FIRST-withholding the unsafe dose immediately protects the child.

opts.text: Verify the child's documented weight and recalculate the safe dose range
opts.rat: SECOND-confirming the weight and math establishes the discrepancy on solid ground.

opts.text: Obtain an independent double-check from a second nurse
opts.rat: THIRD-a second verifier confirms the calculation before escalating.

opts.text: Contact the prescriber and obtain a corrected written order with read-back
opts.rat: FOURTH-the corrected order is obtained and verified to prevent the same error recurring.

opts.text: Administer the corrected dose and document the clarification per policy
opts.rat: LAST-the safe dose is given and the near-miss documented/reported so the system can learn from it.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: Classify each outcome as Improving, Worsening, or Unchanged.

opts.text: The prescriber rewrites the order as 300 mg PO, within the 200-300 mg safe range
opts.rat: A corrected order inside the calculated safe range resolves the original hazard.

opts.text: The child's temperature falls to 99.2F after the corrected dose, with no adverse effects
opts.rat: Fever reduction without adverse effects shows the corrected dose was both safe and effective.

opts.text: A near-miss report is filed and the unit removes trailing zeros from its order sets
opts.rat: System-level change from a reported near miss reduces the chance of the same error reaching another child.

opts.text: A colleague states she gave the 600 mg to a similar child earlier without questioning it
opts.rat: Another child has already received an excessive dose-that child needs assessment and the event needs immediate reporting.

opts.text: The child develops nausea, vomiting, and right upper quadrant pain with rising ALT/AST
opts.rat: These are early signs of acetaminophen-related liver injury and require urgent evaluation and treatment.

opts.text: The prescriber verbally insists the 600 mg is correct but will not enter a written order
opts.rat: An unresolved unsafe order with no written clarification is still a hazard; the nurse should escalate through the chain of command.

opts.text: The child's temperature remains 102.2F one hour after the corrected dose
opts.rat: The fever has not yet responded; this warrants continued monitoring but is not itself a sign of harm.

opts.text: Heart rate remains 122 with the child alert and drinking fluids
opts.rat: A stable heart rate in an alert, hydrated febrile child is neither improvement nor deterioration.
